summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing its most severe electric utility affordability crisis in at least two decades. Between mid-2024 and mid-2025, statewide residential electric bills rose an average of 17.5% — roughly $28 per month — with NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana seeing increases approaching $50 per month. A wave of major rate cases by Duke Energy Indiana, NIPSCO, AES Indiana, and CenterPoint Energy was approved or decided by the IURC during 2025–2026, adding further pressure on households; a landmark June 2026 IURC vote to grant AES Indiana an additional $71 million triggered Governor Braun to replace the IURC chairman and call for rehearing, while the legislature passed HEA 1002 in February 2026 — Indiana's first major electric utility affordability law in over a decade — introducing summer shutoff protections, multi-year rate plans with performance incentives, and automatic levelized billing for low-income households.

messaging_recommendations.message: You have new rights starting this summer: Indiana's new law (HEA 1002) now protects you from electric shutoffs on days forecast to reach a 95-degree heat index, and automatically enrolls you in a levelized billing plan after June 30, 2026 — call your utility or the OUCC at 1-888-441-2494 to confirm your enrollment and ask about bill assistance through the Energy Assistance Program.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Indiana state legislators and the Governor's office
messaging_recommendations.message: HEA 1002 was a critical first step, but Indiana residential customers still face structural barriers: unelected regulators who are not confirmed by the legislature, trackers that bypass full rate cases, and utilities that repeatedly seek returns on equity well above what the OUCC recommends — further statutory reform capping tracker revenue recovery and requiring IURC commissioner confirmation is needed to make affordability durable.

Indiana's five investor-owned utilities have secured rate increases totaling hundreds of millions of dollars since 2024, while the Indiana LIHEAP/EAP program served only about 122,000 households in FY2024 — a fraction of those struggling to pay bills — making the gap between utility profits and household energy burdens a defining kitchen-table issue for Hoosiers heading into the 2026 election cycle.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Community action agencies and faith-based organizations
messaging_recommendations.message: Partner with the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition to help clients understand and act on new HEA 1002 protections: automatic levelized billing, the new summer shutoff moratorium, and the right to petition for rehearing of utility rate orders — and mobilize client voices in IURC listening sessions and public comment processes, which demonstrably influence commission decisions.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana's general rate case requesting a two-phase base rate increase totaling approximately $491.5 million annually. The IURC approved a single-phase increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues, roughly 60% of the original request, effective in two steps in early 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$295.7 million annual revenue (~$19.16/month for 1,000 kWh residential customer); original request was ~$491.5 million

rate_cases.description: The OUCC filed a challenge in June 2026 alleging Duke Energy Indiana over-collected more than $89 million from ratepayers following the 2025 rate order, and is set to over-collect an additional $86 million annually. The OUCC and consumer groups are appealing the IURC presiding officers' finding that Duke's collection was a permissible 'refinement' of the order.
rate_cases.rate_change: Disputed over-collection of ~$89 million (past) and ~$86 million/year (ongoing)

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a regulatory rate review requesting a $192.9 million base rate increase to be phased in as 7.5% in Q2 2026 and 6% in January 2027. A partial settlement was reached with industrial customers and the City of Indianapolis (but not the OUCC or CAC); the IURC voted 3-1 on June 17, 2026 to approve a modified $71 million increase — about 37% of the original request — prompting Gov. Braun to call for rehearing and replace the IURC chairman.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$71 million annually (approx. 3.5–4% residential increase); original request was $192.9 million

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed an electric base rate case in September 2024 requesting approximately $368.7 million in additional annual revenues (originally proposed as ~$32/month for the average residential customer). A settlement agreement reduced the approved increase to approximately $257 million; the IURC approved the settlement on June 26, 2025, phasing in the 16.75% residential increase from July 2025 through Q1 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: +~$257 million annually (+$23/month or 16.75% for average residential customer using 672 kWh/month); original request was ~$368.7 million

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy's electric utility filed for a $118.8 million (16.02%) base rate increase in December 2023. The IURC approved a settlement on February 3, 2025 authorizing approximately $80 million in additional annual revenues, implemented in two phases, with the second phase effective in early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: +~$80 million annually (two-phase implementation; ~$11.18/month in first phase)

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO's petition to create a subsidiary (GenCo) intended to structurally isolate large-load and data-center-related generation costs from general residential and commercial ratepayers. The IURC approved the proposal on September 24, 2025. NIPSCO and GenCo filed a verified joint petition in April 2026 for alternative regulatory plans under the order.

rate_cases.utility: Indiana (statewide — all five investor-owned electric utilities)
rate_cases.case_number: IURC Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability (no formal cause number)
rate_cases.description: On February 24, 2026, IURC Chairman Andy Zay announced a formal Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, convening a daylong hearing on March 24, 2026, at which Indiana's five investor-owned electric utilities (Duke, NIPSCO, AES Indiana, CenterPoint, and Indiana Michigan Power) presented on affordability, transparency, growth, and rate design. The IURC announced a second phase of the inquiry following the hearing.

articles.headline: State regulators approve $70M increase for AES, less than utility originally sought
articles.summary: The IURC voted 3-1 on June 17, 2026, to approve a $71 million electricity rate increase for AES Indiana customers — approximately 37% of the utility's original $192.9 million request and below a partial settlement with industrial customers. OUCC Counselor Abby Gray called the order 'an outrage,' and Governor Braun publicly called it 'unacceptable,' asking the OUCC to petition for rehearing. The decision triggered a broader leadership shake-up at the IURC days later.

articles.headline: 'We mean business on keeping rates down': Governor Mike Braun ousts IURC Chairman following $71 million AES Indiana rate increase
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun replaced IURC Chairman Andy Zay with Commissioner Anthony Swinger — a 25-year veteran of the OUCC — citing Braun's disappointment with the commission's approval of the AES rate increase. Swinger vowed to 'heavily scrutinize any requests that come in from utilities.' The governor also called for a rehearing of the AES rate case and indicated the move was a signal to other investor-owned utilities.

articles.headline: Braun replaces chairman of the Utility Regulatory Commission days after AES rate-hike decision
articles.summary: Six months after appointing former state senator Andy Zay as IURC chairman, Gov. Braun removed him and installed Commissioner Anthony Swinger, effective June 22, 2026. Zay will remain on the commission as a regular commissioner. Critics, including the Citizens Action Coalition, cautioned that personnel changes alone will not lower utility bills without broader statutory reform.

articles.headline: State's ratepayer advocate accuses Duke of overcharging customers in appeal to regulators
articles.summary: The OUCC filed a challenge accusing Duke Energy Indiana of collecting more than $89 million beyond what the IURC authorized in its 2025 rate order, and projected the over-collection at an additional $86 million per year going forward. Counselor Abby Gray said consumers 'deserve to have affordability at the forefront of every issue considered before the Commission.' The OUCC and CAC filed an appeal with the Indiana Court of Appeals, which could take several months to resolve.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers will see a temporary increase in bills this summer
articles.summary: AES Indiana customers faced an additional $3.98/month fuel adjustment charge (FAC) for June through August 2026, stemming from higher-than-forecast fuel costs during Winter Storm Fern in January 2026. The OUCC did not oppose the temporary increase, and the full IURC approved it unanimously. This came on top of the pending base rate case decision, highlighting how tracker charges compound base rate increases for residential customers.

articles.headline: Indiana now blocks utility shutoffs on the hottest days — but only for some customers
articles.summary: Indiana's new HEA 1002 law prohibits privately and cooperatively owned electric utilities from disconnecting residential service within 48 hours of a forecast heat index of 95°F or higher — but only for customers enrolled in the federal energy assistance program. Consumer advocates celebrated the new protection as a long-sought safeguard, especially given rising heat-related health risks, but noted it leaves non-EAP-enrolled vulnerable customers without coverage.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: IURC Chairman Andy Zay announced a formal Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability at a February 25, 2026 press conference, calling in Indiana's five largest investor-owned electric utilities to a March 24 daylong hearing. Zay said the balance between utility and customer interests feels 'dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers.' The inquiry marked the first formal regulatory proceeding specifically targeting electric affordability in Indiana's recent history.

articles.headline: Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities
articles.summary: Indiana's HEA 1002, signed by Gov. Braun on February 26, 2026, introduces multi-year rate plans with performance-based incentives, a summer shutoff moratorium tied to heat advisories, automatic levelized billing for low-income EAP-enrolled customers after June 30, 2026, and new transparency requirements for utility bills. The law is the first major electric utility affordability reform in Indiana in over a decade. Consumer advocates called it a 'good start' while pushing for stronger measures.

articles.headline: How Indy's rising electricity bills stack up
articles.summary: AES Indiana customers in central Indiana faced multiple converging rate pressures in 2026: a base rate case requesting 13.5% increases in two phases, previously approved tracker charges adding roughly 6% more, and a summer fuel adjustment surcharge. The OUCC had recommended a rate reduction rather than an increase. Residential customers in Indianapolis have seen their bills climb sharply compared to state and national averages.

articles.headline: AES rate hikes hit struggling Hoosiers
articles.summary: Following the IURC's June 17 approval of a $71 million rate increase for AES Indiana, consumer advocates and low-income residents described the financial hardship caused by compounding rate hikes. Citizens Action Coalition Executive Director Kerwin Olson said the situation is 'a call for more policy from the Indiana General Assembly' and pointed to the lack of direct accountability for unelected IURC commissioners. The article documented residents skipping food and medicine to afford utility bills.

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The AES rate order issued today is an outrage and Hoosiers deserve better! Governor Braun has made it clear that ratepayer affordability is a priority, far more than just a 'hot topic.'" (Exact quote from press statement.)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / WFYI Public Media news articles

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "Duke has recovered and will continue to recover over $86 million a year more than what was approved. This is not 'refinement' — just ask the Hoosiers footing the bill." (Exact quote from OUCC filing/media statement.)
stakeholders.source: WISH-TV / Indiana Capital Chronicle, state's ratepayer advocate accuses Duke of overcharging

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement." (Exact quote from OUCC press release.)
stakeholders.source: The Indiana Lawyer / Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "My top priority is affordability, which is why I am deeply disappointed by the IURC's approval of another AES rate increase. Hoosiers have spent years tightening their belts and making tough financial decisions. It's time for utility companies to do the same." (Exact quote from governor's public statement.)

stakeholders.name: Anthony Swinger
stakeholders.title: Chairman (newly appointed), formerly Commissioner
stakeholders.quote: "What I'm going to do is make sure that we heavily scrutinize any requests that come in from utilities; they have the burden of proof in these cases, and it behooves the regulators to have a healthy skepticism as we look at any such request." (Exact quote from media interview.)

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Former Chairman (now Commissioner)
stakeholders.quote: "That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry. We're asking our five investor-owned utilities that we regulate on a week-in, week-out basis … to come to this room and have an open discussion." (Exact quote from press conference.)

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "It's the same old, same old. And from where I sit, this situation is a call for more policy from the Indiana General Assembly. We remain one of the very few states where our regulators are not directly elected by the public or confirmed by one or both chambers of the General Assembly. These regulators are effectively unaccountable for the public interest." (Exact quote.)
stakeholders.source: Axios Indianapolis, 'AES rate hikes hit struggling Hoosiers'

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "We've heard the right things, but the actions have not matched those words, and consumers can't take much more." (Exact quote, regarding the IURC leadership change.)
stakeholders.source: Fox 59, Governor Braun ousts IURC Chairman article

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is at a crisis point when it comes to our electric utility bills." (Exact quote.)

stakeholders.name: Brian Latham
stakeholders.title: Electric Division Director
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: AES' case-in-chief "appears to simply give lip service to affordability while focusing on shareholder returns." He noted that residential customers pay 25% more in 2025 than in 2023. (Paraphrased introduction, with the quoted portion in quotation marks being exact.)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Indiana Public Media, September 2025 OUCC testimony filing
